title: Handling Long Documents
concept: BERT has a 512-token limit; classifying long documents requires chunking the text, pooling predictions, or using long-context models.

exercises.prompt: How many tokens can standard BERT handle in a single input?
exercises.explain: Standard BERT has a maximum limit of 512 tokens per input sequence.

cards.title: BERT's Short Memory
cards.body: Standard BERT models are incredibly smart, but they have a memory limit: they can only process 512 tokens at a time. What happens when you want to classify a whole book chapter or a long legal contract? Let's find out how to break through this barrier!

cards.title: What is the Token Limit?
cards.body: BERT has a strict limit of 512 tokens (roughly 400 words) per input. To classify longer documents, we must use techniques like chunking (breaking the text down), pooling (combining predictions from each chunk), or switching to special long-context models designed for longer texts.

cards.title: The Magnifying Glass
cards.body: Imagine trying to read a giant scroll through a small magnifying glass that only shows 512 words at a time. To understand the whole story, you can't look at it all at once. Instead, you slide the glass along, reading one chunk at a time, and then summarize what you saw across the entire scroll.

cards.title: Chunking in Action
cards.body: If you want to analyze a 1,500-token blog post, you can split it into three chunks of 500 tokens each. You pass each chunk through BERT separately to get three sentiment scores, and then average those scores to get the final sentiment for the whole blog post.

cards.title: Can't We Just Make it Bigger?
cards.body: You might think you can just increase BERT's input size setting to 10,000 tokens. However, standard BERT's attention mechanism scales quadratically, meaning doubling the length requires four times the computing power. Trying to run huge lengths on standard BERT will crash your system!

cards.title: Direct vs. Chunked Input
cards.body: Standard BERT struggles with long texts, but we can solve this by changing how we feed the text into the model.

right.text: Splitting the 2,000-word essay into four smaller parts and analyzing each one individually.

left.text: Feeding a 2,000-word essay directly into standard BERT. The model cuts off most of the text.

exercises.prompt: What is the maximum token limit for standard BERT models?
exercises.explain: Standard BERT models have a maximum input limit of 512 tokens due to the way their memory and attention mechanisms are designed.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you send a 1,000-token document to standard BERT without chunking it, BERT will cut off and ignore the second half.
exercises.explain: If a document is longer than 512 tokens, standard BERT will truncate (cut off) the extra text, meaning it will completely ignore the end of your document unless you chunk it first.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of processing long documents.
exercises.sentence: To analyze a long document with BERT, we first split the text into smaller [blank] of 512 tokens or less.
exercises.explain: Because standard BERT cannot accept more than 512 tokens at once, we must break long documents into smaller chunks.

exercises.prompt: An AI assistant is asked to analyze the sentiment of a long 2,000-word essay using BERT. Which assistant response shows the correct way to do this?

options.text: AI: "I will split the long article into 512-token parts, analyze each part, and average the scores."
AI: "I will change BERT's settings to process 10,000 tokens at the same time to save time."
AI: "I will just delete everything after the first 512 words and classify only the beginning."

exercises.explain: Option A is correct because it correctly describes chunking and pooling. Option B is incorrect because we cannot simply change a setting to bypass BERT's mathematical limits. Option C is poor practice because it throws away most of the text.

exercises.prompt: You need to classify the sentiment of a 3,000-word book review using BERT. Which approach is the best way to handle this long document?

options.text: Split it into 512-token chunks, run BERT on each, and average their scores
Force BERT to accept all 3,000 words at once without changing its settings
Only read the first 512 words and delete the remaining 2,480 words
Convert the text into a single giant image and use a computer vision model

exercises.explain: BERT has a strict built-in limit of 512 tokens. To handle long documents, you must break the text into smaller chunks, get predictions for each chunk, and average (pool) those predictions to find the overall sentiment.
